Description:
Hello,

My mother has recently been accepted for a level 3 care package and we are looking for a provider.
She is still fairly mobile but had a fall a couple of years ago and broke her hip. Our house is 2 stories and the bathroom is on the second floor.
We have to walk with her up the stairs to shower, she showers by herself. However, she has made a few comments lately about it getting more difficult.
We would be looking at mainly getting some assistance with her getting up and down the stairs and also getting in and out of the shower.
She also has a very limited social circle and we would like to get her more company, as she is often alone at home.
We have a downstairs toilet, but there is a big step down into the laundry area (230mm) and then a step up(210mm) into the toilet. We are wanting to get the floor in this area raised or a false floor put in and also to get the toilet expanded to also contain a standalone shower so she won’t have to use the stairs.
Is this type of work, or part thereof also covered by home maintenance to help stay in the home under care package level 3?

 

Thanks,
Toorone
Client has already signed up with Lumia Care and will start services from 26/1/2026.

Arthur was telling me he had OT visiting, he would need rails in the bathroom, I’m gonna try to contact them to see if they are actioning this, as well as query speech therapy.

 

 

Arthur likes to make his own decisions and maintain his independence as much as possible. He just needs more time to express himself with his speech being affected post stroke.

 

 

Thank you

Hi Mel,

 

I hope you’re going well.

 

I’m writing to ask about your team’s capacity to support a new NDIS client in Mooroolbark.

 

The client (Stan) is in his early 60s, a history of psychosocial disability (anxiety/depression) and MND diagnosed 3 months ago. He appears to be progressing quickly – ambulant with the aim of a frame and 1:1 assist but increasingly reliant on wheelchair transfers, mild-moderate slurring in his speech and emerging swallowing difficulty, upper limb weakness impacting on self-care.

 

The idea at this stage would be to have support services engaged to assist with self-care (bathing, dressing, toileting) as his wife is currently managing everything on her own. I think we’d be looking at engaging a day or two per week and building up from there.

 

If you do have availability, I was hoping it would be possible to arrange an initial meeting sometime before Christmas. Let me know if this would be possible and what your team’s availability is like – I would aim to attend the meeting myself.

 

Kind Regards,

Level 3 HCP - Physiotherapy, clinical care

Email from Nurse Amy at Huon  Hi Stephanie,

 

I have a resident staying with us for a trial run in the Huon Park Lodge, very likely staying permanently, that needs some private care/social support. His daughter has asked me to assist in finding out availability and pricing.

 

His name is Richard Allman, not our usual clientele as he’s 56yrs old. He has an acquired brain injury from radiotherapy treatment in 2008 for a brain cancer. He’s recovered from the cancer but been left with mobility issues and short-term memory loss. Uses a 4WW and falls occasionally but can get himself up pretty well. Starting physio soon to assist with strengthening up, as spends a lot of time bored, sitting around and goes back to bed a lot in the day. Used to be quite physically active prior to the brain injury. We are currently supervising with showers but he’s understandably very uncomfortable having our younger female carers come into the bathroom to assist so declines help. Occasionally lets one of our more mature carers in to help.

 

I have suggested a male carer twice a week for 2 hours to start with, for shower assist, hair wash, shave, assist with deodorant, remind to clean teeth, make sure he puts on clean clothes etc. He either forgets some hygiene steps or can’t manage to complete the task properly himself due to mobility issues and being afraid of falling.

He would also like some social support, possibly to go out to the shops (he’s a smoker and goes to buy cigarettes once a week) and then out for coffee. He said he used to enjoy playing snooker, we have a table in our park club building (next one along from the lodge) so a short walk down there and a game maybe? Things like that to start with. He’s a bit depressed with the other residents here being quite a bit older than him, so if there was a male carer available that he had more in common with and could have a chat, he’d like that.

 

His daughter Monique (in her early 20’s) is his NOK and has asked for some assistance from me to organise this support. She currently has the paperwork ready to submit to NDIS for financial assistance for services, but in the meantime, she would like a quote on price to forward to Richard’s financial advisor, to see if starting up care prior to NDIS starting/being approved and paying out of pocket would be financially feasible. If/when it is approved, would he be able to claim services from Lumia? I’m not too up to speed on how NDIS works.  

 

I’m out of the office after today until Sunday, so I’ll follow up with you next week.

 

Many thanks,

 

Amy

 

Nursing Unit Manager

 

 

Huon Park

Hi Lumia Care,

My name is Eden and I am a Support Coordinator for a NDIS participant who is potentially looking to engage your support services.

The participant is a 45 year old female with a diagnosis of a progressive and permanent neurological condition and is bed bound as a result of her condition.
As the participant is bed bound, she is physically unable to initiate tasks. Simple actions such as getting out of bed or reaching for an item, are not possible without assistance. This physical dependant and often cognitive impairment, makes it nearly impossible for her to initiate tasks without verbal request. Her condition requires her to depend entirely on others for all activities of daily living (ADLs);

Due to the participants disability, the participant is double incontinent and requires full carer assistance to complete personal hygiene, apply necessary creams and oils and change her continence aids as well as clothing and bed linen.  Likewise, the participant requires complex bowel care including full assistance with suppositories, flushing, monitoring and following procedures to reduce risk of infection.
The participant is at high risk of pressure sores, requiring support workers to frequently and consistently ensure property pressure care and prevention due to her condition.
The participant requires support workers/carers to store and administer her medication as required. Additionally, her carers pick up her medication from the pharmacy when needed. 
The participant requires full assistance with feeding/eating/drinking, with often episodes of choking and vomiting. As a result, food must be prepared and cut specifically to suit the participants needs

The participant has a strong NDIS plan within her CORE funding, however she does not have specific funding for Nursing services.

Can you please kindly advise if your service can provide support from experienced employees who would have experience in the above listed care requirements, whilst understanding the payment rate would not be at a nursing service rate?

Please let me know if you require any further information to discuss this further.

Many thanks,
Eden

NURSING SPINAL PLAN OF CARE 17.12.24
Naomi Hnaialum
Rehab CNC (Walley, K)
Plan of Care reviewed by CNC weekly or more often as indicated.
 
Naomi has been known to the Victorian Paediatric Rehabilitation Service at the Royal Children's Hospital since September 2024. She is under the medical care of The Victorian Paediatric Rehabilitation Service.
 
Naomi was admitted to the Royal Children's Hospital on 27/9/2024 following a Motor Vehicle Accident resulting in C4 fracture, C6-7 burst fracture, Multiple level Cervical Spine decompression + instrumentation + fusion.  
Classification of ASIA B,
NLI C5
Sensory R (C7), L (C5)
Motor R C8,  L C7
 
As a result of the accident, Naomi has associated issues of tetraplegia (paralysis below the level of her neck with inability to move all 4 limbs and trunk), reduced sensation below the level of the neck, neurogenic bladder and bowel, and temperature instability. Formal ASIA completed Monday 14th October 2024.
 
Naomi requires ongoing rehabilitation and has high care needs as a result of her injury.
 
Naomi is at risk for postural hypotension when moving from supine to sitting upright. This is due to loss of sympathetic autonomic nervous system innervation and includes an inability to regulate BP normally with vasoconstriction. Naomi is occasionally experiencing dizziness and nausea on movement - has improved since commencing midodrine.
 
 
To avoid problems with postural hypotension:
Anti embolic stockings and/or SCCD’s will encourage venous return from the legs. Above knee TEDS with thanks and wear at all times.  Check for pressure under the stockings once per shift or more.
 
Abdominal binders encourage venous return through the IVC -   Please ensure binder is on for all transfers and when in elevation of 30 degrees or higher.
 
Increase bed/chair angles in one fluid motion - currently tolerating sitting.
 
Bed resting lower limb splints (BRAFOs) 2 hours on/ 2 hours off during the day and off overnight.  Please ensure that Naomi's heel is sitting all the way back into the AFO to prevent lifting.
 
Respiratory:  Breath stacking/cough assist
 
Cough Assist Instructions:
   - To be provided by trained individuals at the request of Naomi
   - Cough assist machine and circuit at the bedside
   - Utilise Preset 2: MI-E (Auto Cough Trak), +20/-26, 1.6ins: 1.5exs, 1x cough, +/- yankeur suction, then repeat as requested by Naomi  
Family NOT trained in administration
 
Bladder Care:
FG12 intermittent catheter - Please use N/Saline for wash + lubricant for insertion.
Catheter times:  1200, 1500, 1800, 2200
Please leave last CIC in situ - tape to leg with micropore and attach to large drainage bag. Catheter to remain on free drainage overnight - remove in AM (0800)
 Naomi will have an indwelling catheter for the purpose of the visit.
 *Please ensure that the catheter tubing is not kinked and catheter is draining well when on free drainage.  
Please empty drainage bag as needed.
 
Please monitor for signs of sediment in the tubing - milk tube to assist with drainage.  Any obstruction to IDC can increase chance of Autonomic Dysreflexia
 Please document strict output (pad volume, CIC and any spontaneous output please)
 

Bowel Care Routine:
Encourage regular evening bowel routine.
Daily (PM) enema
Movicol, ideally microlax 20 mins after meal up in commode and over toilet when able
 
 
Skin care and hygiene:
Please continue to provide twice daily skin checks to check for any pressure areas.  Please encourage Naomi to pressure relieve self when on visit.
 Please ensure that a full body skin check occurs  BD - apply sorboline to any broken down or dry areas along with consideration of pressure relieving dressing (mepolex border) and document any change or skin breakdown.  
Please perform full skin check BD
Please check skin more often when Naomi is up in chair and returns to bed.
 
 
Transfers and positioning: Currently transferring via hoist x 2  -Plan for visit is that she will transport in chair.
 
Hoist transfer assist x 2 with medium sling,blue loops at the top and red at the bottom
 
Naomi able to offer some support in relation to trunk rotation in preparation for rolling.
 
 
Sitting out in PWC as requested
 
Bed mobility = moderate assistance x 2 with slide sheets
Rolling = min-mod Ax2, Naomi assisting with rolling
Sitting up in bed = using bed mechanics, as far as tolerated
Bed to wheelchair =Hoist AX2 (blue loops for top red for bottom) standard sling. Support given to head and neck and lower limbs. Naomi can now use controls to support her participation in transfer
Power wheelchair = close SV, prompting at times to slow down in chair and keep speed low indoors
 
 
Thermoregulation:
Encourage Naomi to wear light weight layered clothing when able - to assist with temperature control.
 
Autonomic Dysreflexia (AD): Please monitor closely for signs
Naomi has commenced training in how to recognise the signs ad symptoms for AD  and how to ask for help.
Monitor for signs of Autonomic Dysreflexia (AD)
Please monitor for: Persistent hypertension, tachycardia, tachypnea, red blotchy rash, thumping headache, blurred vision, increased anxiety.
   
The first line of treatment of autonomic dysreflexia is to identify and remove the cause
 
     Remove any splints, remove any restrictive clothing including socks, shoes, pants, etc
     Perform Catheter - check tubing and flow
     Check bowel and empty if necessary
     If pressure is the predicted reason adjust positioning (in line with spinal precautions)
     Administer analgesia for any pain
 
Symptoms should resolve immediately once noxious stimulus removed.
 
If the cause cannot be found or the symptoms persist after the perceived cause has been removed then please initiate call to 000

Thanks so much, I look forward to hearing from you ASAP.

Appreciate your help.
Kindest Regards,
Kate
